title: Isaiah 43
subtitle: When You Pass Through the Waters, I Will Be with You

sections.header: Verses 1–13 — “You Are Precious and Honoured in My Sight”

heb.gloss: to redeem / to act as kinsman-redeemer
heb.paragraph: "Do not fear, for I have redeemed (gāʾal) you; I have summoned you by name; you are mine" (v.1). THE chapter of divine tenderness. "When you pass through the waters, I will be with you; and when you pass through the rivers, they will not sweep over you. When you walk through the fire, the flames will not set you ablaze" (v.2). Water and fire: the two universal destroyers, both neutralised by divine presence. "You are precious and honoured in my sight, and I love you" (v.4). Nations are given as ransom in exchange for Israel (v.3). "Do not be afraid, for I am with you" (v.5). From east, west, north, south — God gathers everyone called by his name (vv.5–7). "I, even I, am the LORD, and apart from me there is no saviour" (v.11).

notes.note: "I have redeemed you; I have summoned you by name; you are mine. When you pass through the waters, I will be with you." The three claims: redeemed, named, possessed. The three promises: waters, rivers, fire. The presence-promise is not prevention but accompaniment.

"You are precious and honoured in my sight, and because I love you." The most direct declaration of divine love for Israel in the OT. Precious, honoured, loved. Three words that define the relationship.

"You are my witnesses. Before me no god was formed, nor will there be one after me. I, even I, am the LORD, and apart from me there is no saviour." Monotheism stated absolutely. Israel as witness to the only God.

refs.note: The called-by-name and precious-in-my-sight language undergirds Paul's golden chain of election and glorification.
Peter applies the chosen-people language to the church, echoing Isaiah's affirmation of redeemed identity.
The 'fear not, I am with you' formula recurs as the characteristic assurance of Deutero-Isaiah.
The psalm's imagery of passing through fire and water parallels Isaiah's promise of divine protection through catastrophe.

hist.context: The salvation oracle (43:1–7) is among the most intimate in the Hebrew Bible. YHWH addresses Israel by name, claims ownership by creation and redemption, and promises presence through water and fire — not exemption from trial but accompaniment through it. The ransom language (v.3) is striking: Egypt, Cush, and Seba are offered as payment for Israel's release. Historically, this may allude to Cyrus's conquest of Egypt (which occurred after his release of the exiles). The chapter establishes that Israel's value is not earned but bestowed.

sections.header: Verses 14–28 — “Forget the Former Things; I Am Doing a New Thing!”

heb.paragraph: "Forget the former things; do not dwell on the past. See, I am doing a new thing (ḥădāshâ)! Now it springs up; do you not perceive it? I am making a way in the wilderness and streams in the wasteland" (vv.18–19). God promises Babylon’s fall (v.14). Then the stunning command: forget the Exodus! The new thing surpasses the old. "The people I formed for myself that they may proclaim my praise" (v.21). But Israel has not called on God; they have burdened him with sins and wearied him with offences (vv.22–24). "I, even I, am he who blots out your transgressions, for my own sake, and remembers your sins no more" (v.25). Forgiveness for God’s own sake — not because of Israel’s merit but because of God’s character.

notes.note: "Forget the former things! See, I am doing a new thing!" The command to forget the Exodus is revolutionary. The new exodus will so surpass the first that the first becomes forgettable.
"The people I formed for myself that they may proclaim my praise." Purpose statement: Israel exists FOR praise. The national purpose is doxological.

"I, even I, am he who blots out your transgressions, for my own sake, and remembers your sins no more." For MY sake. Forgiveness is not earned but flows from God’s own character. He remembers sins no more — divine forgetting as grace.

refs.note: The new-creation language Paul employs draws on Isaiah's 'forget the former things' announcement.
The eschatological erasure of the old order and declaration 'Behold, I make all things new' echoes Isaiah's new-thing promise.
New heavens and new earth — the later Isaiah oracle expands the new-thing motif to cosmic dimensions.
The new covenant promise in Jeremiah parallels Isaiah's vision of a fresh divine act superseding the old exodus.

hist.context: The famous 'forget the former things' oracle (vv.18–19) announces that the coming exodus will eclipse even the original deliverance from Egypt. Rivers in the desert and water in the wilderness will mark YHWH's new act. Yet the chapter also contains a devastating indictment: Israel has burdened YHWH with sins rather than honoring him with sacrifices (vv.22–24). The juxtaposition is theological whiplash — election and accusation, promise and rebuke. YHWH blots out transgressions for his own sake, not because Israel deserves it (v.25).

ppl.text: I, even I, am the LORD, and apart from me there is no saviour…

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: " The most direct declaration of divine love for Israel in the OT…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVAll the nations gather together and the peoples assemble. Which of their gods foretold this and proclaimed to us the former things? Let them bring in their witnesses to prove they were right, so that others may hear and say, “It is true.”ESVAll the nations gather together, and the peoples assemble. Who among them can declare this, and show us the former things? Let them bring their witnesses to prove them right, and let them hear and say, It is true.NIVWhen you pass through the waters, I will be with you; and when you pass through the rivers, they will not sweep over you. When you walk through the fire, you will not be burned; the flames will not set you ablaze.ESVWhen you pass through the waters, I will be with you; and through the rivers, they shall not overwhelm you; when you walk through fire you shall not be burned, and the flame shall not consume you.

rec.text: ""I have redeemed you; I have summoned you by name; you are mine. When you pass through the waters, I will be with you." The three claims: redeemed, named, possessed. The three promises: waters, rivers…"

rows.label: A New Thing — 43:14–28
rows.text: "FORGET the former things! I am doing a NEW THING!" "I blot out your sins FOR MY OWN SAKE and remember them NO MORE."

hebtext.gloss: to redeem / to act as kinsman-redeemer
hebtext.note: "Do not fear, for I have redeemed (gāʾal) you; I have summoned you by name; you are mine" (v.1). THE chapter of divine tenderness. "When you pass through the waters, I will be with you; and when you pass through the rivers, they will not sweep over you. When you walk through the fire, the flames will not set you ablaze" (v.2). Water and fire: the two universal destroyers, both neutralised by divine presence. "You are precious and honoured in my sight, and I love you" (v.4). Nations are given as ransom in exchange for Israel (v.3). "Do not be afraid, for I am with you" (v.5). From east, west, north, south — God gathers everyone called by his name (vv.5–7). "I, even I, am the LORD, and apart from me there is no saviour" (v.11).

"Forget the former things; do not dwell on the past. See, I am doing a new thing (ḥădāshâ)! Now it springs up; do you not perceive it? I am making a way in the wilderness and streams in the wasteland" (vv.18–19). God promises Babylon’s fall (v.14). Then the stunning command: forget the Exodus! The new thing surpasses the old. "The people I formed for myself that they may proclaim my praise" (v.21). But Israel has not called on God; they have burdened him with sins and wearied him with offences (vv.22–24). "I, even I, am he who blots out your transgressions, for my own sake, and remembers your sins no more" (v.25). Forgiveness for God’s own sake — not because of Israel’s merit but because of God’s character.

themes.note: Mercy peaks at 10: precious, honoured, loved. Waters, rivers, fire. Sins blotted for God’s own sake. Covenant at 9: redeemed, named, mine. The new thing surpasses the Exodus.

coaching.tip: 'When you pass through the waters, I will be with you' (v. 2). God doesn't promise absence of fire and flood but presence within them. The preposition matters: through, not around. This verse became the anthem of persecuted communities because it redefines safety — not as avoidance of suffering but as companionship within it. Immanuel theology in its most practical form.

'Forget the former things; do not dwell on the past. See, I am doing a new thing!' (vv. 18–19). God tells Israel to stop clinging to the Exodus — the defining event of their identity. This is radical: the God who performed the Exodus says 'that's not the ceiling.' The new thing surpasses even the old miracle. Isaiah's theology is progressive — God's best work is always ahead.

prayer_prompt: As God declares 'When you pass through the waters, I will be with you,' consider asking Him to remind you of a past deliverance that proves He will be faithful in your current flood.
